does: This paper systematically evaluates whether sparse autoencoders (SAEs) successfully reduce polysemanticity in large language models by introducing a quantitative Jensen-Shannon distance-based concept separability score. The authors compare SAEs against base models across two large language models (Gemma-2-2B and DeepSeek-R1) and multiple SAE variants using five datasets spanning word-level and sentence-level contexts. Their core contribution is demonstrating that SAEs produce higher concept separability scores and achieve more monosemantic representations than dense activations. The evaluation includes practical assessments of concept-level interventions through full neuron masking and other strategies, providing empirical evidence that SAEs genuinely transform polysemantic neurons into more interpretable, concept-specific features.
connects: This work directly addresses longstanding challenges in mechanistic interpretability of neural networks, building on prior research documenting polysemanticity as a major obstacle to understanding model behavior. It extends recent proposals of sparse autoencoders as a solution to this problem by adding rigorous quantitative evaluation where prior work relied on qualitative observations. The paper connects to the broader literature on feature learning and disentanglement in deep learning, drawing methodologically from information-theoretic approaches to measuring concept separation. It also engages with intervention-based interpretability methods that probe model components to understand their functional roles, combining these techniques with SAE-based representation transformation to create a comprehensive evaluation framework.
enables: This work establishes a principled methodology for assessing interpretability improvements in neural networks, opening pathways for developing more sophisticated sparse autoencoders optimized explicitly for monosemanticity. The concept separability score provides a quantitative metric for comparing representation learning approaches across models, enabling systematic research into which SAE architectures and training objectives best reduce polysemanticity. The paper's findings support using SAEs as a foundation for downstream mechanistic interpretability research, including more reliable circuit discovery, causal interventions, and safety applications. This quantitative framework could also generalize to other representation-learning techniques, and the intervention results suggest SAEs could improve model editing and control strategies by operating on cleaner, more interpretable feature spaces.
